content.full_text:
,_.
1.’
a11rorn1a
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 6,
1992
Joe Pandit
Director, District One
Santa Clara Valley Water District
5750 Almaden Expressway
San Jose,
CA 95118
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92-l72
Dear Mr.
Pandit:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding your duties and responsibilities under the conflict—of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Our
jurisdiction is limited to the provisions of the Act.
Accordingly,
we make no comments regarding other provisions of law
that might be applicable to your facts,
such as the doctrine of
incompatible offices.
We suggest that you consult with the Office
of the Attorney General regarding other applicable provisions of
law.
QUESTIONS
1.
Do the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act require
you to disqualify yourself from participating in a decision of the
Santa Clara Valley Water District to grant you leave without pay?
2.
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
you to disqualify yourself from participating in a decision to
pass a resolution which would grant a leave of absence without pay
to any employee of the Santa Clara Valley Water District who is
elected to the district’s board of directors?
3.
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
prohibit your employment with the Santa Clara Valley Water
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660


Our File No. A-92-172
Page
2
District while you serve on the board of directors of the
district?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not
require you to disqualify yourself from participating in a
decision to grant you leave without pay.
2.
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not
require you to disqualify yourself from participating in a
decision to grant a leave of absence without pay to any employee
of the district elected to serve on the district’s board of
directors.
3.
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not
prohibit your employment with the district while you serve on the
board of directors of the district.
However, other provisions of
law might prohibit such simultaneous employment.
‘ACTS
You are a member of the board of directors (the “board”)
of
the Santa Clara Valley Water District (the “district”),
a special
district formed by legislative act.
You were first elected to
your position as director in November,
1982,
and have served in
that capacity since January,
1983.
Prior to your election to the position of director,
you were
employed with the district as an engineer for a period of thirteen
and one—half years.
Before taking the oath of office,
you
requested the board of directors of the district to grant you
leave without pay from your employment for the time you would
serve as a director.
The district board denied your request.
You
assumed the office of director but did not resign your position as
an engineer with the district.
You state in your request for advice that in 1990 two cases
similar to your situation were tried in court.
In both cases, the
court ruled that the employees had the right to retain their
employee status while sitting on the board of directors of a
public entity.
Based upon those rulings,
you requested the
district board to reconsider its decision and grant you leave
without pay as you had previotisly requested.
The board reviewed
the case law and agreed that you should have been granted the
leave.
At this time, the board is interested in granting you a leave
of absence from your position as an engineer for the district.
You are concerned that, because you are a member of the board of
directors of the district, if the board were to grant you a leave
of absence at this time there might be a violation of the
conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act.


Our File No. A-92-172
Page
3
To avoid conflict—of--interest issues, the board has
considered adoption of a resolution providing that any past or
present employee who is elected to serve on the board of directors
of the district would be granted leave without pay status for the
duration of his or her tenure on the board and would be reinstated
as a full time employee in the same or equivalent position at the
end of the employee’s tenure on the board of directors.
In addition,
in a letter dated March 31,
1992, received by
this office on April 14,
1992,
you ask whether the board of
directors could reinstate you as a full time employee prior to the
end of your tenure as a member of the board of directors.
ANALYSIS
Question
1
The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating
in,
or using their official position to influence a governmental
decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a
financial interest.
As a member of the board of directors of the
Santa Clara Valley Water District,
a public entity,
you are a
public official subject to the provisions of the Act.
(Section
82048.)
Financial Interests
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
2 or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
2
An official’s “immediate family” includes his spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No. A-92-172
Page
4
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103,
footnote added.
Accordingly,
you may not make, participate in making,
or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on your economic interests specified in Section
87103 above.
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your
economic interests will be materially affected by a governmental
decision,
you must not only disqualify yourself from participating
in formal decisions of the board of directors which may affect
such interests, but you must also abstain from attempting to
influence such decisions by communicating with other members of
the board of directors or with the staff regarding the decisions.
Prior to 1982,
when you were elected to the board of
directors of the district, you were an employee of the district.
You have asked whether you may participate in a decision of the
board to grant you leave without pay so that you may resume your
employment with the district upon completion of your term of
office as a director.
The Act provides a number of exceptions to the general rule
discussed above.
For example Section 82030(b) (2)
excludes from
the definition of income any salary and reimbursement for expenses
or per diem received from a state, local,
or federal government
agency.
Therefore,
any salary which you may receive from the
district would not constitute a disqualifying economic interest.


Our File No. A-92-172
Page
5
In addition, Regulation 18700(d) (3) states that making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision shall not
include actions by public officials,
employees,
or employee
representatives relating to their compensation or the terms or
conditions of their employment or contract.
Accordingly,
you may
participate in decisions of the board to grant you leave without
pay from your previous employment as an engineer for the district.
Question
2
You have also asked whether you may participate in a decision
to pass an ordinance that would grant leave without pay to any
employee of the district who is elected to serve on the board of
directors.
As stated above,
a public official is not attempting
to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision when the official negotiates his or her compensation or
the terms and conditions of his or her employment or contract.
(Regulation 18700.1(b) (3).)
Although Regulation 18702.1(a) (4)
prohibits a public official from participating in any decision
which would foreseeably increase or decrease the personal
expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities of the official or the
official’s immediate family by $250, this regulation also provides
a narrow exception which states that:
(c)
Notwithstanding subsection
(a)
an
official does not have to disqualify himself or
herself from a governmental decision if:
(1)
The decision only affects the
salary, per diem,
or reimbursement for expenses the
official or his or her spouse receives from a state
or local government agency.
This subsection does
not apply to decisions to hire, fire,
promote,
demote,
or discipline an official’s spouse,
or to
set a salary for an official’s spouse which is
different from salaries paid to other employees of
the spouse’s agency in the same job classification
or position;
Regulation 18702.1(c) (1)
Thus, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would
not preclude your participation in a decision of the board of
directors to adopt a resolution that any past or present employee
of the district who is elected to serve on the board of directors
will be granted leave without pay status for the time during which
the employee serves on the board of directors and will be
reinstated as a full time employee in the same or equivalent
position when the employee ceases to serve on the board of
directors.
This is so because the provisions of the Act do not
prohibit the participation of public employees in matters relating
to the terms and conditions of their employment contract.


Our File No. A-92-172
Page
6
Please note,
however, that the exception in Regulation
18702.1(c)
does not apply to decisions to hire, fire, promote,
demote,
or discipline you,
or to set a salary for you which is
different from salaries paid to other employees of the district in
the same job classification or position.
(Carrillo Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—431.)
question
3
You have also asked whether you may be reinstated as a full
time employee of the district while you continue to serve on the
district’s board of directors.
As stated above,
salary received
from a government agency is exempted from the definition of
“income.”
(Section 82030(b).)
Accordingly,
as an employee of the
district,
your salary would be exempted from the definition of
income under the Act.
(Jenke Advice Letter,
No.
A—83-00l; Carison
Advice Letter,
No.
A—90—472.)
Moreover,
as stated above,
Regulation 18700(d) (3)
allows your participation in decisions
regarding your compensation or the terms or conditions of your
employment or contract.
(Carrillo, supra.)
Therefore,
you would
not be prohibited from participating in a decision of the board to
reinstate you as an employee of the district while you continue to
serve as a member of the district’s board of directors.
Other provisions of law,
however, deal with the propriety of
simultaneously holding positions that might impose incompatible
responsibilities on a public official.
These provisions are not
included in the Act.
The Commission does not provide advice
regarding provisions of law other than the Act.
Accordingly,
you
should contact the Office of the Attorney General for advice
regarding other applicable provisions of law.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Blanca M.
Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division
SH:BMB:bb
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not require you to disqualify yourself from participating in a decision to grant a leave of absence without pay to any employee of the district elected to serve on the district’s board of directors. 3. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not prohibit your employment with the district while you serve on the board of directors of the district. However, other provisions of law might prohibit such simultaneous employment. ‘ACTS You are a member of the board of directors (the “board”) of the Santa Clara Valley Water District (the “district”), a special district formed by legislative act. You were first elected to your position as director in November, 1982, and have served in that capacity since January, 1983. Prior to your election to the position of director, you were employed with the district as an engineer for a period of thirteen and one—half years.
